briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. I will now begin the climate briefing for May 16, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Global Climate Crisis Warnings and Call for Public Health Emergency
sections.content: Today, an independent Pan-European Commission on Climate and Health, convened by leading international experts and the World Health Organization (WHO), strongly urged the WHO to declare the climate crisis a global public health emergency [11, 32]. This acknowledges the severe threat climate change poses to human health, with researchers from the University of Montreal warning that climate change is exacerbating respiratory distress [24]. The United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) also warned that climate change could push one million Ghanaians into poverty by 2050, making it clear that the climate crisis is not merely an environmental issue but also a socioeconomic and public health concern [30]. These warnings indicate that climate change is a complex crisis requiring urgent global-level responses.

sections.title: ⚠️ Ecosystem's Cry: River Oxygen Depletion and Domestic and International Climate Impacts
sections.content: Reports from scientists continue daily, indicating a decrease in oxygen levels in rivers worldwide [12, 15, 16, 18, 22, 23, 25, 26]. This phenomenon, where rivers are 'suffocating' due to climate change, is a serious issue that threatens aquatic ecosystems and can lead to a reduction in biodiversity [16, 26]. Domestically, there have been reports that the climate crisis has transformed a previously mild May into a 'tyrant' [1], and seasonal changes, such as the disappearance of muddy seasons in specific regions, are being detected [14]. Furthermore, Korea's beekeeping industry is severely impacted by climate change, imports, and labeling confusion [20], and analyses on the impact of climate change on floods are underway [21], demonstrating that various ecosystems and industries worldwide are directly affected by climate change.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Republic of Korea, Steps Towards a Carbon-Neutral Society
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts are continuing for the transition to a carbon-neutral society. The Presidential Committee on Climate Crisis Response is active [3], and Democratic Party candidate Kim Sung-bum announced a pledge to link carbon neutrality with tangible welfare benefits, presenting a future of 'Clean Seogwipo' through energy welfare [2, 6]. In local governments, Ansan mayoral candidate Lee Min-geun declared carbon neutrality as a core value [4], and Gyeonggi Province is accelerating its energy transition, ushering in an era of 10GW renewable energy led by experts [10]. The participation of civil society, businesses, and educational institutions is also active, including the ESG tree-planting event by National Hoengseong Forest Recreation Center and Hansung Motor [5], environmental cleanup activities by the Naechon Red Cross Volunteer Group in Hongcheon-gun [8], and the completion of Sehan University's 'Carbon Emission Assessor' training program [9], leading to the spread of a new welfare culture in carbon neutrality practices [7].

sections.title: 🌍 International Community's Climate Response Efforts and Technological Advancements
sections.content: The international community is working in various ways to address climate change. Environment and Climate Change Canada and ArcelorMittal Mining Canada demonstrated responsible corporate action by reaching an agreement to invest over $400 million to address the issue [17, 29]. In Wellington, New Zealand, the voice of civil society is also growing louder, with student protesters marching to Parliament to demand climate action [28]. Meanwhile, technology is playing a crucial role in combating climate change and building a sustainable future [19], and specific technological solutions are being discussed, such as the decarbonization cost for data centers applying carbon capture technology to gas power generation, which is estimated at $115 per MWh [31]. The India Meteorological Department (IMD) announced its plan to change heatwave declaration parameters in response to the climate change crisis [27], indicating that climate adaptation efforts in various countries are also continuing.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the international community's warnings about the severity of the climate crisis, along with various carbon neutrality practices and adaptation efforts underway both domestically and internationally. While climate change has far-reaching impacts, we have confirmed that hope for a sustainable future can be built through technological innovation, political will, and the participation of civil society. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.original_title: Declare climate crisis a global public health emergency, experts tell WHO
articles.description: Exclusive: Commission says alert would trigger coordinated international response that could help avoid millions dyingThe climate crisis should be declared a global public health emergency by the World Health Organization, or millions more people will die unnecessarily, leading international experts have said.The independent pan-European commission on climate and health, which was convened by the WHO, concluded the climate crisis was such a worldwide threat to health that the WHO should declare

summary.phenomenon: Leading international experts and the independent pan-European commission on climate and health, convened by the WHO, urged the World Health Organization (WHO) to declare the climate crisis a global public health emergency.
summary.cause: This call was made due to the conclusion that the climate crisis poses a global threat to health.
summary.outlook: Such a declaration could trigger a coordinated international response, helping to prevent millions of unnecessary deaths.

articles.original_description: Exclusive: Commission says alert would trigger coordinated international response that could help avoid millions dyingThe climate crisis should be declared a global public health emergency by the World Health Organization, or millions more people will die unnecessarily, leading international experts have said.The independent pan-European commission on climate and health, which was convened by the WHO, concluded the climate crisis was such a worldwide threat to health that the WHO should declare
